nativeRange.habitat: The Clown Trigger inhabits clear coastal and outer-reef environments across a depth range of 1 to 75 metres. Adults are typically encountered along steep seaward drop-offs in open water, while juveniles tend to shelter inside small caves on invertebrate-rich reef walls.

decorPreferences: large caves and overhangs for sleeping and retreat

diet.notes: Wild diet consists of sea urchins, crustaceans, molluscs, and tunicates. In captivity, generally advised to feed a varied meaty diet — chopped squid, krill, clams in the shell, silversides, hard-shelled shrimp, and mussel — 1–2 times per day. Hard-shelled foods are important to wear down the continuously-growing teeth.

goodWith: other large triggerfish (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: conspecifics (no two Clown Triggers in the same tank)
slow-moving fish such as seahorses, mandarins, and most blennies

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by H. Zell; 4752x3168. Tack-sharp, vibrant adult with full body in frame showing all diagnostics: large white belly spots, yellow-orange lips, yellow reticulated dorsal saddle with black spots, yellow snout band.
Photo by Alexander Fordyce; 2048x1536. Sharp in-situ wild adult over reef coral, whole fish, all pattern features clearly visible.
Photo by Justin Philbois; 2048x1536. In-situ reef scene with blue sea star, colorful whole-fish shot; slight underwater haze, kept as variety filler.

summary: The Clown Trigger (Balistoides conspicillum) is a boldly patterned Indo-Pacific species widely considered among the most aggressive fish commonly available in the marine trade. It requires a very large tank, robust rockwork, and carefully chosen tankmates.
careNotes: The Clown Trigger is one of the most iconic — and most demanding — fish in the marine hobby. Juveniles are deceptively docile but tend to become increasingly territorial and aggressive as they mature past roughly 20 cm, and adults can reach about 50 cm. The species is widely considered not reef-safe: in the wild it preys on crustaceans, molluscs, and urchins, and in captivity it has been known to nip corals, rearrange rockwork, and uproot decor. A very large tank with both open swimming space and large caves for sleeping and retreat is generally advised; published minimum recommendations range from around 500 to 1140 litres, and the conservative choice for a full-grown adult is around 1000 litres with a tank footprint of at least 180 cm. For adult specimens a species-only arrangement is often the safest option — if tankmates are attempted, they should be similarly large and assertive (large angelfish, groupers, tangs, pufferfish, other large triggerfish), and even then aggression flares are common. Only one Clown Trigger should be kept per system. Strong filtration is needed to handle the heavy, meaty diet. Hard-shelled foods such as clams in the shell, krill, and hard-shelled shrimp help wear down the continuously-growing teeth.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquaria; spawning is occasionally reported in very large public-aquarium systems. Sexual dimorphism is subtle and no reliable indicator has been established for hobby identification.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary retailer care card: 300 gal min, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025, aggressive, not reef safe, 20" adult size.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy (Balistidae / Tetraodontiformes), 50 cm max TL, depth 1–75 m, temperature 23.3–28.8 °C, Indo-Pacific range East Africa to Samoa. Used as authoritative source for taxonomy and wild ecology because Reef2Reef wiki page 403'd.

additional.name: AquariumSource
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 120 gal absolute min / 200+ gal recommended (lower than LiveAquaria's 300 gal), 74–80°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, lifespan 8 yr wild / up to 20 yr in captivity, behavioural note that juveniles become hostile past ~8 inches. Spread on minimum tank size was wide (≈450–1140 L) — biased to the larger end (1000 L) per playbook tank-size rule because the species is very large and very aggressive.

additional.name: Aquatic Community
additional.notes: Secondary husbandry confirmation: pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, 22–26 °C, minimum 500 L cited, not reef-safe, single specimen only.
